channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: This MIT robot flies through the air then dives underwater using the same wings - TechSpot
item.description:
1. This MIT robot flies through the air then dives underwater using the same wings  TechSpot
  2. A new kind of robot swims the seas and soars the skies  NPR
  3. New flapping robot swims and flies like a diving bird  MIT News
  4. This 250-Gram Robot Can Swim Underwater Then Fly into the Sky Using Its Flapping Wings Like a Diving Bird  ZME Science
  5. MIT researchers study avian mechanics to build robot that can dive, swim and fly  Silicon Republic

item.title: FCC approves first Reflect Orbital satellite - SpaceNews
item.description:
1. FCC approves first Reflect Orbital satellite  SpaceNews
  2. F.C.C. Approves Test of Space Mirror to Light Night Sky Despite Outcry  The New York Times
  3. The U.S. just approved a giant space mirror to test ‘sunlight on demand.’ Low Earth orbit is getting weird  The Conversation
  4. FCC Approves Extremely Controversial Space Mirror Satellite That Could "Flash Blind" Drivers And Ruin Astronomy  IFLScience
  5. In 2026, the FCC authorized radio operations for Reflect Orbital’s first 18-metre space mirror, a single demonstration for a proposed 50,000-satellite system that one astronomy model says could raise the natural night-sky background by 200% to 300%  Space Daily

item.title: SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket launches for 35th time, hauls Starlink satellites to orbit (video) - Space
item.description:
1. SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket launches for 35th time, hauls Starlink satellites to orbit (video)  Space
  2. Here’s when Vandenberg’s rocket launch will be visible in Central Valley  yourcentralvalley.com
  3. Need Friday night plans? Catch tonight’s SpaceX launch from these SoCal spots  KTLA
  4. SpaceX launches 24 Starlink satellites on Falcon 9 rocket from Vandenberg SFB  Spaceflight Now
  5. SpaceX stock in focus as launch, Starlink business hit new milestones  Yahoo Finance

item.title: Rare Planetary Parade On July 12: Moon To Appear Alongside Mars, Saturn And Uranus - NDTV
item.description: Rare Planetary Parade On July 12: Moon To Appear Alongside Mars, Saturn And Uranus  NDTVSee more headlines & perspectives on Google News

item.title: In July 2026, Europe's Euclid telescope found 31 of the oldest quasars ever seen, two now shining with the light of a trillion suns when the cosmos was just 5 percent of its present age — their glow 13 billion years in the coming - Space Daily
item.description:
1. In July 2026, Europe's Euclid telescope found 31 of the oldest quasars ever seen, two now shining with the light of a trillion suns when the cosmos was just 5 percent of its present age — their glow 13 billion years in the coming  Space Daily
  2. ESA’s Euclid Space Telescope Finds Universe’s Most Ancient Quasars  NASA Science (.gov)
  3. Scientists have discovered the oldest quasar ever seen, and it shines with the light of a trillion suns  Space
  4. Ancient quasars add to a 'major unsolved problem' in astrophysics  Reuters
  5. Astronomers have found 31 of the oldest known quasars burning so soon after the Big Bang that current models struggle to explain how their black holes grew massive enough to power them in the time available  ScienceBlog.com

item.title: Titan has rivers, rainfall, lakes and a slow hydrological cycle exactly like Earth's, except that every drop of it is liquid methane and the rock the rivers run over is water ice - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Titan has rivers, rainfall, lakes and a slow hydrological cycle exactly like Earth's, except that every drop of it is liquid methane and the rock the rivers run over is water ice  Space Daily
  2. The rivers on Titan flow, pool into lakes and are fed by rainfall on a slow cycle much like our own, but the liquid is methane and the ground beneath it is water frozen hard as stone  Space Daily

item.title: Planets, meteor shower, and a Buck Moon: Astronomical events for July 2026 - WDSU
item.description:
1. Planets, meteor shower, and a Buck Moon: Astronomical events for July 2026  WDSU
  2. This Week's Sky at a Glance, July 10 – 19  Sky & Telescope
  3. July celestial events you won't want to miss  WGME
  4. Capture the Night: ProMaster’s Summer 2026 Astrophotography Guide  PetaPixel
  5. Starwatch: The great summer stars take center stage  Pottsville Republican Herald

item.title: If you compressed the entire history of Earth into a single calendar year, humans appear at 11:36 pm on the 31st of December, agriculture at 11:59, and everything written down in history occupies the final 15 seconds before midnight - Space Daily
item.description: If you compressed the entire history of Earth into a single calendar year, humans appear at 11:36 pm on the 31st of December, agriculture at 11:59, and everything written down in history occupies the final 15 seconds before midnight  Space Daily

item.title: China’s Tianwen-2 Space Probe Has Rendezvoused With Earth’s Quasi-Moon - WIRED
item.description:
1. China’s Tianwen-2 Space Probe Has Rendezvoused With Earth’s Quasi-Moon  WIRED
  2. In Deep Space, China Meets Earth’s ‘Amazing’ Quasi-Moon  The New York Times
  3. Tianwen-2 arrives at asteroid Kamo’oalewa, first image revealed  SpaceNews
  4. China releases 1st photo of Earth's elusive 'quasi-moon' Kamo'oalewa  Space
  5. Chinese spacecraft Tianwen-2 beams back first image of Earth’s “mini moon”  Scientific American

item.title: Scientists discover new seafloor being born — for the first time in history: ‘Massive event’ - New York Post
item.description:
1. Scientists discover new seafloor being born — for the first time in history: ‘Massive event’  New York Post
  2. In a First, Scientists Observe Creation of New Seafloor  The New York Times
  3. Anatomy of a seafloor spreading event captured by in situ seismogeodesy  Nature
  4. World First: Scientists Witnessed a Piece of Earth's Oceanic Crust Being Born  ScienceAlert
  5. Scientists just caught a glimpse of Earth’s biggest game of The Floor Is Lava  Scientific American

item.title: A single rare meteorite has revealed the chemical fingerprint of a Moon-sized world that once orbited the young Sun before being destroyed in a catastrophic collision — a lost planet science never knew existed until it landed as a rock - Space Daily
item.description: A single rare meteorite has revealed the chemical fingerprint of a Moon-sized world that once orbited the young Sun before being destroyed in a catastrophic collision — a lost planet science never knew existed until it landed as a rock  Space Daily

item.title: Bats can catch and eat birds mid-flight. A painter may have known that 400 years before scientists - CBC
item.description:
1. Bats can catch and eat birds mid-flight. A painter may have known that 400 years before scientists  CBC
  2. Scientists Just Learned That This Bat Eats Birds Midflight. A Renaissance Painter May Have Known About It Hundreds of Years Ago  Smithsonian Magazine
  3. The hidden animal mystery inside a 1611 painting has finally been solved  The Indian Express
  4. Renaissance Painting May Show Animal Behavior Only Recently "Discovered", A 10-Kilometer Comet Is About To Make A Return, And Much More This Week  IFLScience
  5. Does This 400-Year-Old Renaissance Painting Show An Animal Behavior Only ‘Discovered’ In 2025?  IFLScience

  5. The ghost in Orion's shell: Hydrogen maps show repeated stellar feedback sculpted around Orion Nebula  Phys.org

item.title: The real-life 'Hobbits' survived on an Indonesian island for thousands of years, until a long drought may - The Times of India
item.description:
1. The real-life 'Hobbits' survived on an Indonesian island for thousands of years, until a long drought may  The Times of India
  2. Hobbit-like human relatives may have been on a less advanced evolutionary path  CNN
  3. ‘Hobbits’ likely scavenged dragons’ kills  Science News
  4. Flores Hobbits’ eating habits offer clues about their evolutionary past  Ars Technica
  5. The “Hobbits” Mysteriously Disappeared 50,000 Years Ago – Scientists Have Revealed What Happened to Their Home  SciTechDaily

item.title: Saturn’s moon Enceladus is spraying a hidden ocean into space — and inside that spray is the same chemical fuel that feeds life in Earth’s deepest oceans - Space Daily
item.description: Saturn’s moon Enceladus is spraying a hidden ocean into space — and inside that spray is the same chemical fuel that feeds life in Earth’s deepest oceans  Space Daily

item.title: Nearly a millennium ago sky-watchers recorded a new star so bright it was visible in daylight for weeks, and Hubble has now traced how far its remnant, the Crab Nebula, has expanded since — a thousand-year-old explosion still visibly unfolding - Space Daily
item.description: Nearly a millennium ago sky-watchers recorded a new star so bright it was visible in daylight for weeks, and Hubble has now traced how far its remnant, the Crab Nebula, has expanded since — a thousand-year-old explosion still visibly unfolding  Space Daily

item.title: When a deep-sea robot ventured down into the Atlantic's Doldrums, it found something massive no one expected – leaving scientists stunned - BBC Wildlife Magazine
item.description:
1. When a deep-sea robot ventured down into the Atlantic's Doldrums, it found something massive no one expected – leaving scientists stunned  BBC Wildlife Magazine

  4. New measurement brings us closer to understanding the Universe’s rate of expansion  Technology Org
  5. Yes, the Universe’s Expansion Is Still Accelerating, Researchers Say  The Japan News

item.title: In March 2026, a strange 'chirp' from a distant dying star proved to be something never seen so clearly before: the birth of a magnetar — a newborn neutron star so magnetic it could wipe every credit card on Earth from halfway to the Moon - Space Daily
item.description: In March 2026, a strange 'chirp' from a distant dying star proved to be something never seen so clearly before: the birth of a magnetar — a newborn neutron star so magnetic it could wipe every credit card on Earth from halfway to the Moon  Space Daily

item.title: James Webb has caught at least five galaxies colliding about 800 million years after the Big Bang, flinging heavy elements out into space far earlier than any model predicted — evidence the early universe was already messy and mature when it should still ha - Space Daily
item.description: James Webb has caught at least five galaxies colliding about 800 million years after the Big Bang, flinging heavy elements out into space far earlier than any model predicted — evidence the early universe was already messy and mature when it should still ha  Space Daily

item.title: We get humans to Mars. What then? Here's what we'll really do on the Red Planet – and why it could solve the greatest mystery in science - BBC Sky at Night Magazine
item.description:
1. We get humans to Mars. What then? Here's what we'll really do on the Red Planet – and why it could solve the greatest mystery in science  BBC Sky at Night Magazine
  2. TERRY TURNER: Mars or bust? Bust.  Moultrie Observer

item.title: The rocks at the very summit of Mount Everest are limestone formed on an ancient ocean floor, and fossils of trilobites, sea lilies, and tiny crustaceans have been found in samples collected just six metres below the top of the highest point on Earth - Space Daily
item.description: The rocks at the very summit of Mount Everest are limestone formed on an ancient ocean floor, and fossils of trilobites, sea lilies, and tiny crustaceans have been found in samples collected just six metres below the top of the highest point on Earth  Space Daily

item.title: Does fast charging damage your battery more than regular charging? - Live Science
item.description: Does fast charging damage your battery more than regular charging?  Live Science

item.title: NASA is keeping Voyager 1 alive by switching off instruments and heaters one by one—but it cannot let the spacecraft become so cold that its fuel lines freeze. If those lines fail, Voyager could lose the thrusters that keep its antenna aimed at Earth, permanently - Space Daily
item.description:
1. NASA is keeping Voyager 1 alive by switching off instruments and heaters one by one—but it cannot let the spacecraft become so cold that its fuel lines freeze. If those lines fail, Voyager could lose the thrusters that keep its antenna aimed at Earth, permanently  Space Daily
  2. The Voyager 1 probe, launched in 1977, is now so far from Earth that a radio signal travelling at the speed of light takes over 22 hours to reach it, and it is still sending data back from interstellar space on a power source the size of a car battery  Space Daily
  3. Voyager Spacecraft: The Ultimate Power Management Challenge?  EE Times
  4. 50 Years Later, NASA's Voyager Probe Is Reaching A Big Milestone For Space Travel  Yahoo
  5. Voyager 1 is now so far away that its plutonium power source is quietly fading, and sometime in the 2030s NASA will switch off its final instrument, ending the only signal ever sent to us from interstellar space.  ScienceBlog.com

item.title: Bacteria turn dissolved uranium into stable compound in 130 days, study finds - Phys.org
item.description: Bacteria turn dissolved uranium into stable compound in 130 days, study finds  Phys.org

item.title: Science news this week: Time emerges inside a mini-universe, scientists thicken Arctic ice, and one of the oldest graves of a free Black person in the US found - Live Science
item.description:
1. Science news this week: Time emerges inside a mini-universe, scientists thicken Arctic ice, and one of the oldest graves of a free Black person in the US found  Live Science

  3. 'Time was speeding up, slowing down, or even stopping': Physicist demonstrates a key theory of time by buil...  Yahoo
  4. What if time isn't real? Physicists built a tiny quantum universe with 24,000 ultracold atoms and found a shocking truth: Time could create itself  The Times of India
  5. Physicists Watched Time Invent Itself Inside a Cloud of 24,000 Ultracold Atoms  TechEBlog -

item.description: Could permanent magnets protect astronauts from solar storms?  Phys.org

item.title: We tend to assume life needs a star to begin, but a 2025 study found that some moons carried into deep space by planets expelled during supernova explosions could preserve subsurface oceans for billions of years — heated not by sunlight, but by the repeat - Space Daily
item.description:
1. We tend to assume life needs a star to begin, but a 2025 study found that some moons carried into deep space by planets expelled during supernova explosions could preserve subsurface oceans for billions of years — heated not by sunlight, but by the repeat  Space Daily
  2. Astronomers believe the Milky Way may contain billions, perhaps even trillions, of rogue planets drifting without close stars — and a 2026 study found one 9,800 light-years away that was likely born in a planetary system before being thrown into the dark  Space Daily

item.title: In June 2026, the James Webb telescope read the dawn and dusk skies of WASP-121b separately and found one side hotter and stripped of water, the other cooler and possibly cloudy, on a world where it can rain liquid rock - Space Daily
item.description: In June 2026, the James Webb telescope read the dawn and dusk skies of WASP-121b separately and found one side hotter and stripped of water, the other cooler and possibly cloudy, on a world where it can rain liquid rock  Space Daily

item.title: A metal detector led a prospector to a rock that resisted a saw, acid, drill, and sledgehammer; years later, scientists cut the block with diamond and discovered a 17 kg meteorite from the beginning of the Solar System. - CPG Click Oil and Gas
